originalTitle: Girl, Interrupted review – mental health memoir reborn as patchy Aimee Mann-soundtracked musical
summary: Susanna Kaysen’s retelling of her time in a psychiatric hospital in the 60s became an Oscar-winning movie in the 90s and now it’s an elegant, if limited, play
content: In 2021, the singer-songwriter Aimee Mann (perhaps best known, as a solo artist, for her contributions to the Paul Thomas Anderson film Magnolia) released an album called Queens of the Summer Hotel, a collection of somewhat narrative tunes inspired by Susanna Kaysen’s bestselling memoir of mental health struggle, Girl, Interrupted. Mann had been commissioned to write the songs for a stage adaptation that took years (and the duration of a pandemic) to materialize. Now finally at the Public Theater in Manhattan, Girl, Interrupted is a sturdy showcase for Mann’s gorgeous, shimmering-sad compositions, but perhaps a less successful conduit for Kaysen’s arguments. The press materials for Girl, Interrupted are careful to stress one point of distinction: this is a “play with music”, not, strictly, a musical. The former term has been around for a long time, but eludes exact definition. It’s a “know it when you see it” (or hear it) kind of a thing. Girl, Interrupted, though, fits rather comfortably under the rubric of the traditional musical. The songs may not advance the plot, exactly, but they do offer crucial insight into the inner lives of the troubled young women encountered by a fictionalized Susanna at McLean, a storied psychiatric hospital in suburban Boston. Susanna (Juliana Canfield) arrives there in 1967, having survived a suicide attempt (aspirin chased with vodka was her method of choice) and needing, in the polite euphemism of the day, a “rest”. She is immediately introduced to an array of young women with differing ailments: charismatic Lisa (King Princess) has been diagnosed a sociopath; Susanna’s roommate Grace (Mia Pak) is a stable-seeming person who has schizophrenia; Daisy (Katherine Reis) has obsessive issues related to eating; Polly (Sally Shaw) is covered in burn scars from having lit herself on fire. These characters will be familiar to those who’ve seen James Mangold’s 1999 film adaptation (for which Angelina Jolie, who played Lisa, won an Oscar). But this stage version has tamped down much of the movie’s cinematic overstatement: here, the characters dwell much closer to Earth. Which might have played somewhat flat were it not for Mann’s songs, spare but poetic arias and duets that let us drift inside the minds and hearts of these passionate, confused people. Particularly striking are Burn It Out, in which Polly attempts to explain the thinking behind her horrific act of self-mutilation, and Robert Lowell and Sylvia Plath, sung by Susanna and Grace as they contemplate the fine line between creative genius and madness, evoking two famous former McLean patients. Each song is sung in rich and melancholy tones, accompanied by varying combinations of piano, violin and guitar (some of which are played by the actors). Girl, Interrupted is an austere chamber piece, and yet its moments of music briefly access a vastness of abstract thought. Director Jo Bonney’s staging is simple, judiciously employing a turntable and a few chairs. (Heather Gilbert’s lighting design helps create individual spaces and moods.) At times one yearns for more energy, some showwomanship that might turn up the temperature. But Bonney and playwright Martyna Majok are determined in their restraint. The end result is that we maybe don’t feel the weight of Kaysen’s conclusions about her experience as powerfully as we should. Any deeper meaning is kept at a remove, delicate and feathery in the middle distance. What lessons or ideas we are meant to take away from the piece are very much ours to suss out afterward. Still, Canfield – who worked such play-with-music wonders two seasons ago in Stereophonic – does her noble best to illuminate Susanna’s condition. She holds the center of the play with a quiet, intelligent firmness, selling us on the production’s reflective, half-dispassionate conceit. This is adult Susanna looking back at a moment in her life that she still does not fully comprehend. All she knows for certain is that there was woefully little understanding of, or genuine interest in, the inner lives of young women at that time, a fact she confronts with a sort of wry resignation. King Princess, a recording artist as well as an actor, takes the flashiest role and shrewdly underplays it. Quite unlike Jolie’s lupine, prowling take on the character, this Lisa is calmer, more fellow traveler than domineering alpha. But we do feel her magnetic pull nonetheless; it is not hard to grasp why she is so often the focus of the group’s attention and devotion. And yet, I would like to know Lisa, and all these fascinating people, a bit better – when they are not singing, anyway. Girl, Interrupted is elegant, often engaging theater. But it lilts through its world rather than diving into it. Perhaps the calculation was that Mann has done enough to deepen the story through song. In that case, this is more “music with a play attached” than the other way around.
chineseTitle: 《女孩，中断的评论》——心理健康回忆录重生为不完整的艾梅·曼配乐音乐剧
chineseSummary: 苏珊娜·凯森 (Susanna Kaysen) 重述了 60 年代在精神病院的时光，成为 90 年代的一部奥斯卡获奖电影，现在它是一部优雅但有限的戏剧
chineseContent: 2021 年，创作型歌手艾梅·曼（Aimee Mann）（或许作为独唱艺术家，因对保罗·托马斯·安德森（Paul Thomas Anderson）的电影《木兰花》（Magnolia）做出的贡献而闻名）发行了一张名为《Queens of the Summer Hotel》的专辑，该专辑收录了一些叙事性的曲调，灵感来自苏珊娜·凯森（Susanna Kaysen）关于心理健康斗争的畅销回忆录《女孩，中断》（Girl, Interrupted）。曼恩受委托为舞台改编创作歌曲，该改编花了数年（以及大流行的持续时间）才得以实现。现在，《女孩，中断》终于在曼哈顿的公共剧院上演，它有力地展示了曼华丽、闪烁着悲伤的作品，但也许是凯森论证的一个不太成功的渠道。 《女孩，被打扰》的新闻材料小心翼翼地强调了一个区别：这是一场“音乐剧”，而不是严格意义上的音乐剧。前一个术语已经存在很长时间了，但没有确切的定义。这是一种“看到就知道”（或听到）的东西。不过，《被打扰的女孩》很适合传统音乐剧的标题。确切地说，这些歌曲可能不会推进情节，但它们确实提供了对虚构的苏珊娜在波士顿郊区一家传奇精神病医院麦克莱恩遇到的陷入困境的年轻女性的内心生活的重要洞察。苏珊娜（朱莉安娜·坎菲尔德饰）于 1967 年抵达那里，她曾试图自杀（她选择用阿司匹林配伏特加服用），并且需要（用当时礼貌的委婉说法）“休息”。她立即​​被介绍给一群患有不同疾病的年轻女性：魅力十足的丽莎（国王公主）被诊断为反社会者；苏珊娜的室友格蕾丝（米娅·帕克饰）是一位看似稳定的人，却患有精神分裂症。黛西（凯瑟琳·雷斯饰）有与饮食有关的强迫症；波莉（莎莉·肖饰）被覆盖
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
